region_of_origin: Mexico (Jalisco state); widely available in Singapore as a street food and casual dining item

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general taco class (FOODON_00002473). Birria tacos are a specific braised-meat variant; no dedicated FoodOn URI exists for birria tacos specifically. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tacos — composite meat and tortilla dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated birria taco entry. A0CKW (tacos, composite) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Birria-specific braising and consomme dipping are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to birria tacos as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of birria tacos consists of 2 tacos, each filled with braised meat and served with a side of consomme broth for dipping, totalling approximately 200 g.

nutrient_source.title: Birria Tacos — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Birria Tacos (cr_id G00010845). Saturated fat and sugar are not reported in the HPB record; these are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef taco (USDA NDB 21340) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Corn tortilla GI analogous estimate — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for birria tacos as a composite dish. GI estimated at 46 based on the dominant carbohydrate source: corn tortillas (GI ~46, low category, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables). Braised meat and fat content further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycaemic load calculated as: GI (46) × carbohydrate per serving (200 g × 15.1 g/100 g = 30.2 g) / 100 = 13.9 g, classified as medium GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 363 mg/100 g (borderline high); partially offset by potassium 229 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit birria tacos to one serving (2 tacos) and request reduced salt in the braising liquid; avoid consuming the consomme dipping broth, which concentrates sodium significantly above the taco itself.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 15.1 g/100 g with low GI (~46) and dietary fibre 3.0 g/100 g attenuating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume birria tacos in controlled portions of 2 tacos per meal, leveraging the low GI of corn tortillas and the fibre content to moderate postprandial glucose; monitor for hidden carbohydrates in the consomme broth from chili peppers.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~3.2 g/100 g and estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g from braised red meat; sodium 363 mg/100 g adds vascular load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit birria tacos to occasional consumption, request lean cuts of beef or substitute goat, avoid the high-sodium consomme broth, and pair with vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid), nightshade ingredients (chili, tomato), and estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g are pro-inflammatory disqualifiers
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid birria tacos prepared with beef or goat due to high arachidonic acid content and nightshade ingredients; if consumed, request chicken or mushroom substitution and avoid the chili-heavy consomme broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Birria tacos are a Mexican street food originating from the state of Jalisco, Mexico, consisting of corn tortillas filled with slow-braised, spiced meat — traditionally goat (birria de chivo) or beef (birria de res) — that has been stewed in a rich sauce of dried chili peppers (guajillo, ancho, pasilla), tomatoes, onion, garlic, and aromatic spices including cinnamon, cumin, and oregano. The tortillas are typically dipped in the rendered fat skimmed from the braising consomme before being pan-fried until crispy, then filled with the tender shredded meat and served alongside a cup of the consomme broth for dipping. The result is a taco with a crispy, slightly oily exterior, deeply savoury and mildly spiced filling, and a rich umami broth accompaniment. Birria tacos have become globally popular and are increasingly available in Singapore at Mexican casual dining restaurants and food trucks. Nutritionally, per 100 g they provide 170 kcal, 6.69 g protein, 9.17 g fat, and 15.1 g carbohydrate, with 3.0 g dietary fibre and 363 mg sodium. The low glycaemic index (~46) from corn tortillas makes them moderately suitable for blood glucose management in controlled portions, though the sodium content warrants caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The red meat base and nightshade ingredients (chili, tomato) make this dish unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

What meat is in birria tacos?

ingredient_synonyms.consomme: clarified braising broth used as dipping sauce

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id G00010845, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and sugar are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef taco reference and documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using corn tortilla as the dominant carbohydrate source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (3.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.9 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and sugar (2.5 g/100 g) are not reported in the HPB Foodo source record. These values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 21340 (taco with beef, cheese, lettuce, tomato and salsa) as the closest approved-source analogue, adapted for the birria preparation profile. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for birria tacos as a composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the level of specificity required; these fields require dedicated laboratory analysis. 3. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate based on corn tortilla GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for birria tacos as a composite dish exists in any approved source. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; birria tacos traditionally use beef or goat which may be halal if slaughtered appropriately, but preparation method and sourcing must be verified per establishment. 5. The input record classified this dish under food_subgroup 'Batter-based dishes'; this has been corrected to 'Meat-filled tortilla dishes' as corn tortillas are masa-based flatbreads, not batter-based preparations. 6. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Western'; this has been corrected to 'Mexican' as birria tacos originate from Jalisco, Mexico. 7. Local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been added as transliterations/translations to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for this dish as listed in the Singapore HPB database with region_tag SG. Chinese simplified: 比利亚玉米饼卷; Chinese traditional: 比利亞玉米餅卷; Malay: Taco Birria; Tamil: பிரியா டாக்கோஸ்; Hindi: बिरिया टाकोस. These are phonetic/descriptive transliterations as no established native-language names exist for this Mexican dish in these languages. 8. All aliases_by_language entries are confirmed present verbatim in the flat aliases array (q8 self-check passed).
